title: Effects of depression genetic risk and household socioeconomic status on emotional behavior and brain development in early adolescence
authors: Campbell, E.M., Morrel, J., Gauderman, W.J., Herting, M.M.
institution: University of Southern California, Los Angeles, CA
statistical_approach: Polygenic risk scores for major depression; ABCD Study; structural and functional MRI; gene × environment interaction models; multivariate analysis linking SES and genetic risk to brain development
abstract: This study examined how polygenic risk for major depression and household socioeconomic status (SES) jointly shape emotional behavior and brain development in early adolescence using the ABCD Study. Polygenic depression risk and low household SES interacted to predict both emotional behavioral outcomes and structural/functional MRI metrics, suggesting that socioeconomic environments moderate genetic vulnerability to depression during a critical period of brain development.

title: Connectome-based predictive modeling of concurrent and longitudinal substance use vulnerability in adolescence
authors: Guassi Moreira, J.F., Allgaier, N., Johnson, S.B., Potter, A.S., Garavan, H., Fair, D.A.
institution: University of Oregon, Eugene, OR; Oregon Health & Science University, Portland, OR
statistical_approach: Connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM); resting-state fMRI; ABCD Study longitudinal data; whole-brain functional connectivity prediction of concurrent and prospective substance use initiation
abstract: Using connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM) applied to resting-state fMRI from the ABCD Study, this study identified functional connectivity profiles predicting concurrent and longitudinal substance use vulnerability in adolescence. Whole-brain connectivity patterns differentiated adolescents at higher versus lower risk, pointing to frontostriatal and frontolimbic circuits as neural markers of adolescent substance use vulnerability.

title: Maternal technoference decreases brain-to-brain synchrony during mother-infant interaction
authors: van den Heuvel, M.I., Mosińska, M., Turk, E., et al.
institution: Tilburg University, Tilburg, Netherlands
statistical_approach: Dual-EEG hyperscanning; inter-brain synchrony (IBS) analysis; mother-infant face-to-face interaction with smartphone technoference condition; linear mixed-effects models
abstract: This dual-EEG hyperscanning study measured brain-to-brain synchrony between mothers and infants during face-to-face interaction, with and without maternal smartphone use (technoference). Maternal smartphone use significantly reduced inter-brain synchrony in mother-infant dyads, linking everyday digital distraction to diminished neural coupling that underpins social learning and emotional development in early infancy.

title: Neural specialization of print processing in second language learning: A longitudinal ERP study of Chinese children learning English
authors: Huang, J., Ariza, J., Huo, J., Lo, J.C.M., McBride, C., Maurer, U.
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong
statistical_approach: Longitudinal ERP design (T1, T2 follow-up); N170/print-specific ERP components; linear mixed-effects models; print tuning index; Chinese children learning English as L2
abstract: This longitudinal ERP study tracked neural print specialization during English second-language (L2) learning in Chinese-speaking children. Results showed progressive tuning of occipitotemporal ERP responses (N170) to English words over the course of L2 learning, indicating that L1 orthographic neural circuits adapt to accommodate L2 print processing, with experience-dependent specialization observed across the study period.

title: Visual Word Form Area demonstrates individual and task-agnostic consistency but inter-individual variability
authors: Mitchell, D., Fuentes-Jimenez, D., Stone, A., Yablonski, M., Yeatman, J.D.
institution: Stanford University, Stanford, CA
statistical_approach: fMRI task-based localizer; individual-level VWFA characterization; split-half reliability analysis; inter-individual variability assessment; reading development cohort
abstract: The visual word form area (VWFA) is central to reading, but its individual consistency versus population variability is debated. This study showed that the VWFA demonstrates high within-individual consistency across tasks and sessions (task-agnostic reliability) yet shows substantial inter-individual variability in location, size, and selectivity. These findings have implications for understanding reading development and dyslexia, underscoring that the VWFA is a consistent individual-level marker but not a fixed population-level region.

title: Neonatal brain MRI to prognosticate neurodevelopmental outcomes in fetal growth restricted infants: a systematic review
authors: Ramdial, K., MacNamara, N., Moldrich, R., Colditz, P., Wixey, J.
institution: University of Queensland, Brisbane, Australia
statistical_approach: Systematic review (PRISMA); neonatal MRI predictors of neurodevelopmental outcomes in fetal growth restriction (FGR); brain volume, cortical maturation, and diffusion MRI; outcome measures: Bayley scales, IQ, motor scores
abstract: This systematic review evaluated whether neonatal brain MRI can reliably predict neurodevelopmental outcomes in infants with fetal growth restriction (FGR). Structural MRI parameters—including brain volume, cortical maturation indices, and white matter diffusion metrics—were assessed across included studies. Results indicate that neonatal MRI abnormalities in FGR infants predict poorer motor, cognitive, and behavioral outcomes, supporting the clinical utility of early MRI prognostication in this high-risk neonatal population.

title: Newborn brain development comparison of plateau and plain regions: insights from quantitative EEG
authors: Zhao, Y., Ze, X., Li, H., et al.
institution: West China Second University Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China
statistical_approach: Quantitative EEG (qEEG); comparative study of 72 newborns from plateau (>2,500 m altitude) vs. plain regions; spectral power analysis; burst suppression ratio; background EEG maturation indices
abstract: This study compared quantitative EEG features between newborns from high-altitude plateau (>2,500 m) and plain regions to characterize the effects of altitude on early brain development. High-altitude newborns showed altered qEEG spectral patterns compared to plain-region controls, with differences in delta-band power and sleep-state organization, suggesting that chronic hypoxia at high altitude modifies postnatal cortical maturation.

title: Association between neonatal brain volume and school-age executive function in children born moderate-to-late preterm
authors: Rossetti, A.C., Pascoe, L., Mainzer, R., et al.
institution: Murdoch Children's Research Institute / Royal Women's Hospital, Melbourne, Australia
age_range: Neonates at scan (0-12 months), followed to school age (7-9 years)
statistical_approach: Neonatal structural MRI (brain volume, regional morphometry) in moderate-to-late preterm (MLP) infants; longitudinal follow-up to school age; multiple regression; executive function outcomes (CANTAB/BRIEF); mediation analyses
abstract: This longitudinal study examined whether neonatal brain volumes in children born moderate-to-late preterm (32–36 weeks' gestation) predict school-age executive function. Smaller neonatal whole-brain and regional volumes were associated with poorer executive function at school age, providing evidence that early brain growth restriction in MLP preterm infants has lasting cognitive consequences beyond those detectable by standard clinical assessment.

title: Bridging the gap: Translating fetal, infant, and toddler neuroimaging insights into clinical practice
authors: Nelson, D.J., Sacks, D., Korom, M., Remec, N., Perkins, A., Cook, J., Stoyell, S., Evans, D., Moser, J., Capparini, C., Inala, S., Huszar, K., Zieff, G., Vernetti, A., Norton, E.S., Wilkinson, C.L., Arichi, T.
institution: Harvard Medical School / Boston Children's Hospital, Boston, MA; King's College London, London, UK
statistical_approach: Narrative/systematic review; clinical translation framework covering MRI, fNIRS, EEG, and multimodal approaches; barriers and facilitators for NICU and outpatient applications; evidence synthesis across fetal, neonatal, and toddler neuroimaging literature
abstract: This review bridges the gap between developmental neuroimaging research and clinical practice by synthesizing current evidence from fetal, neonatal, infant, and toddler neuroimaging studies. The authors describe how MRI, EEG, and fNIRS findings can inform clinical assessment, monitoring, and early intervention for high-risk infant populations. Key barriers and enabling factors for clinical translation are discussed, with a focus on scalable tools for preterm and neurodivergent infants.

title: Children exhibit greater persistence of motor learning-related patterns of hippocampal activity into post-task wake epochs
authors: Van Roy, A., Temudo, A., Taylor, E.K., Koppelmans, V., Hödlmoser, K., Albouy, G., King, B.R.
institution: KU Leuven, Belgium; University of Utah, Salt Lake City, UT; University of Salzburg, Austria
statistical_approach: Multivoxel pattern analysis (MVPA); hippocampal activity pattern reactivation; post-task resting-state fMRI; linear mixed-effects models; N=22 children (7-11 yr) and 23 adults (18-30 yr); motor sequence learning with 5-hour wake interval
abstract: This fMRI study examined whether children show enhanced hippocampal reactivation during waking rest after motor learning. Twenty-two children (7-11 years) and 23 adults completed two sessions of motor sequence learning separated by a 5-hour wake interval. Children showed greater persistence of task-related hippocampal multivoxel activity patterns into post-learning rest compared to adults, suggesting that the childhood advantage in offline motor memory consolidation is supported by enhanced hippocampal reactivation during wakefulness.

title: MRI-based structural development of the human newborn hypothalamus
authors: Yen, E., De Asis-Cruz, J., Rasmussen, J.M.
institution: Children's National Hospital, Washington, DC
statistical_approach: T1-weighted MRI; automated hypothalamic segmentation; N=631 newborns (699 scans); Developing Human Connectome Project (dHCP) cohort; generalized additive models for developmental trajectory; normative growth charts
abstract: Using 699 T1-weighted MRI scans from 631 newborns (27-45 weeks postmenstrual age) from the Developing Human Connectome Project, this study characterized structural development of the human newborn hypothalamus. Hypothalamic volume grew nonlinearly with postmenstrual age, with differential regional growth trajectories. These normative developmental trajectories provide a reference for identifying hypothalamic abnormalities in clinical neonatal populations.

title: Motor Sequence Learning in Children and Adults: Age Differences in the Time Course of Brain Activation and Representational Stability
authors: Hille, M., Wenger, E., Papadaki, A., Fandakova, Y.
institution: Max Planck Institute for Human Development, Berlin, Germany; University of Crete, Heraklion, Greece
statistical_approach: Task fMRI; representational similarity analysis (RSA); fMRI activation time-course analysis; striatal and cerebellar sequence representations; learning-related representational stability; cross-sectional age comparison (children 7-10 yr vs. adults)
abstract: This study used fMRI and representational similarity analysis to examine age differences in the time course of brain activation and representational stability during motor sequence learning in children (7-10 years) and adults. Children and adults differed in the temporal dynamics and neural locus of sequence representations, particularly in striatum and cerebellum. These findings clarify developmental differences in how motor learning systems support sequence acquisition across the lifespan.

title: Altered spectral patterns of aperiodic electroencephalography in autism
authors: Chien, Y.L., Hsieh, M.H., Hsieh, M.Y., Tseng, W.Y.I.
institution: National Taiwan University Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
statistical_approach: Resting-state EEG; FOOOF/specparam aperiodic exponent and offset; group comparison ASD vs. neurotypical controls; canonical correlation analysis linking aperiodic parameters to ASD symptom severity
abstract: Aperiodic EEG activity indexes cortical excitation-inhibition (E/I) balance. This study characterized aperiodic EEG spectra in adolescents and adults with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) compared to neurotypical controls. Participants with ASD showed significantly altered aperiodic exponents and offsets consistent with disrupted E/I balance, and these spectral patterns correlated with ASD severity measures, supporting aperiodic EEG as a biomarker of E/I dysregulation in autism.

title: Brain criticality emerges with developmental shifts in frequency-specific excitation-inhibition balance
authors: Westbrook, A., Avramiea, A.E.
institution: Rutgers University, New Brunswick, NJ; UMC Utrecht, Netherlands
statistical_approach: Resting-state EEG; detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA) for criticality index; FOOOF frequency-band-specific E/I balance; cross-sectional age comparison (10-33 yr); lifespan developmental trajectory analysis
abstract: This EEG study tested whether brain criticality emerges developmentally in relation to frequency-specific excitation-inhibition (E/I) balance. Using resting-state EEG from participants aged 10-33 years, criticality indices tracked developmental changes in frequency-specific E/I ratios. These results suggest the brain approaches criticality as E/I ratios mature during adolescence and early adulthood, linking neural noise dynamics to neurodevelopmental trajectories.

title: The Adaptive Brain Across Development: Age Related Changes in Cortical Adaptation
authors: Moalem, A., Levinson, M., Jaffe-Dax, S.
institution: Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Jerusalem, Israel
statistical_approach: fNIRS; cortical adaptation (neural suppression to repeated stimuli); auditory stimulation paradigm; cross-sectional comparison across infants, children, and adults; hemodynamic response function analysis
abstract: Cortical adaptation — the reduction of neural responses to repeated stimuli — is fundamental to efficient sensory coding and statistical learning. Using fNIRS, this study tracked age-related changes in cortical adaptation across infants, children, and adults. Adaptation strength increased with age, and the temporal dynamics differed between developmental groups, suggesting that the neural mechanisms supporting efficient sensory prediction develop progressively from infancy through adulthood.

title: Infant Brains Tick at 4Hz – Resonance Properties of the Developing Visual System
authors: Baldauf, M., Jensen, O., Köster, M.
institution: University of Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany; University of Oxford, Oxford, UK
statistical_approach: High-density EEG; steady-state visual evoked potentials (SSVEPs) at 2-30 Hz; impulse response function (IRF) from broadband visual stimulation; frequency-domain analysis; resonance frequency quantification
abstract: This EEG study investigated the resonance properties of the infant visual system using high-density recordings in 42 eight-month-old infants presented with visual stimuli flickering at discrete frequencies (2–30 Hz) and broadband sequences. A 4 Hz theta rhythm emerged as the intrinsic resonance frequency of the infant visual system, independent of stimulation frequency. Unlike adults whose visual cortex resonates at ~10 Hz (alpha), the infant brain preferentially processes at 4 Hz, providing a mechanistic account of infant visual oscillatory dynamics and their developmental divergence from adult neural rhythms.

title: Demonstration of periodic and aperiodic EEG reliability between laboratory and clinic settings
authors: Matsuba, M., Chung, H., Said, J., Norberg, J., Nelson, C.A., Wilkinson, C.L.
institution: Harvard Medical School / Boston Children's Hospital, Boston, MA
age_range: Infants, children, and adults (11 infants, 10 children, 15 adults)
statistical_approach: Test-retest reliability (ICC); FOOOF/specparam for aperiodic (exponent, offset) and periodic EEG parameters; parallel laboratory vs. clinical settings; N=36 across three age groups; intra-class correlations; Bland-Altman analysis
abstract: Reliable EEG measurement across settings is essential for clinical translation of neurodevelopmental biomarkers. This study assessed test-retest reliability of periodic (alpha, theta peaks) and aperiodic (exponent, offset) EEG parameters between a research laboratory and a clinical outpatient setting in 36 participants spanning infants, children, and adults. Periodic and aperiodic EEG parameters showed good-to-excellent cross-setting reliability across all age groups, supporting the feasibility of translating laboratory EEG neurodevelopmental biomarkers into clinical practice.

title: Childhood Electroencephalographic Signatures Predict Distinct Developmental Trajectories to Adolescent Anxiety and Depression
authors: Deng, X., Zhou, Y., Song, J., Ai, H., Zhang, Y., Nan, W., Xu, F.
institution: University of Macau, Macau, China
statistical_approach: Longitudinal resting-state EEG; frontal alpha asymmetry; aperiodic exponent; latent class growth analysis (LCGA); prediction of adolescent internalizing trajectories from childhood EEG baseline
abstract: This longitudinal study identified childhood EEG signatures that prospectively predict distinct developmental trajectories toward adolescent anxiety and depression. Using resting-state EEG from children (ages 9-13 years) at baseline, latent class growth analysis of internalizing symptom trajectories revealed that EEG patterns — including frontal alpha asymmetry and aperiodic exponent — significantly discriminated children with high-risk versus low-risk trajectories for adolescent anxiety and depressive outcomes.

title: Graph-Theoretical Analysis of EEG Functional Brain Network Development in Preterm Infants
authors: Li, J., Shan, Y., Ge, M., Peng, T., Jiang, C., Xu, X., Zhang, P., Lin, Y., Liu, M., Zhou, W., Chen, Y., Cheng, G.
institution: Shanghai Children's Medical Center, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Shanghai, China
statistical_approach: Graph-theoretical EEG; weighted phase-lag index (wPLI); graph metrics (small-worldness, clustering coefficient, global efficiency, betweenness); generalized additive models (GAM); linear mixed-effects models (LMM); N=61 preterm infants (25-36 wk GA) including 40 with longitudinal recordings
abstract: This study applied graph-theoretical analysis to EEG recordings to characterize functional brain network development in 61 preterm infants (25–36 weeks gestational age), 40 with longitudinal data. Using weighted phase-lag index and graph metrics across delta, theta, alpha, and beta bands, generalized additive models identified 36 weeks postmenstrual age as a maturation inflection point. Delta-band network reorganization with emergence of parietal hubs was documented, providing a framework for early stratification of preterm infants at neurological risk.

title: Alterations in EEG functional connectivity in preterm infants: A systematic review
authors: Stevenson, N.J., et al.
institution: University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland
statistical_approach: Systematic review (PRISMA); PubMed and Web of Science (inception–March 2025); 24 included studies; six-item EEG-FC quality checklist; narrative synthesis of EEG functional connectivity findings across frequency bands and postmenstrual ages
abstract: This PRISMA-guided systematic review synthesized evidence on EEG-based functional connectivity in preterm infants (<37 weeks gestational age). A search of PubMed and Web of Science identified 24 studies quantifying resting-state connectivity or graph-theory metrics. Functional connectivity patterns varied by frequency band, postmenstrual age, and behavioral state: delta coherence decreased while frontal theta synchrony increased with advancing PMA; active sleep featured focal high-frequency coupling while quiet sleep showed broader low-frequency integration. The review recommends ≥64-channel EEG, longitudinal multimodal cohorts, and strict false discovery rate correction for future studies.

title: Autism subtypes identified using cross-species functional connectivity analyses
authors: Marco Pagani, Valerio Zerbi, Silvia Gini, Filomena Grazia Alvino, Abhishek Banerjee, Andrea Barberis, et al.
institution: Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia (IIT), Rovereto, Italy
statistical_approach: Cross-species fMRI functional connectivity; 20 genetic mouse models of autism; multicenter human dataset (940 ASD + 1036 NT); clustering/subtyping analysis; inter-species comparison
abstract: Using cross-species functional neuroimaging, this study showed that brain dysconnectivity patterns in autism can be parsed into biologically dissociable subtypes. fMRI connectivity alterations in 20 distinct genetic mouse models of autism cluster into hypoconnectivity-dominant and hyperconnectivity-dominant subtypes linked to distinct biological pathways — hypoconnectivity associated with synaptic dysfunction and hyperconnectivity reflecting transcriptional and immune-related alterations. Analogous hypoconnectivity and hyperconnectivity subtypes were identified in a multicenter human fMRI dataset of 940 individuals with idiopathic autism and 1,036 neurotypical individuals, suggesting these represent transdiagnostically relevant neurobiological categories.

title: Neural tracking of biological motion rhythms in early infancy: links to caregiver touch-related behaviours and attitudes
authors: Alicja Brzozowska, Berit Reise, Aneliya Antova, Celine Henning, Stefanie Hoehl
institution: University of Vienna, Vienna, Austria
statistical_approach: Neural entrainment analysis; frequency-domain EEG; linear mixed-effects models; N=32 infants at 3 and 6 months
abstract: This EEG study examined whether the infant brain tracks the rhythmicity of locomotion-related biological motion in the visual domain in 3- and 6-month-olds. Results showed robust neural tracking of biological motion rhythms at both ages, with no effects of age or orientation (upright or inverted). Caregiver-reported practice of infant carrying/babywearing and caregiver attitudes toward social touch were linked to infant neural tracking of biological motion rhythms, particularly in the inverted condition. Exploratory analyses revealed a left-hemisphere lateralization effect for rightward biological motion rhythms.

title: Neuroanatomical differences in early bilingual and monolingual children
authors: Mia R. Coutinho, Guinevere F. Eden
institution: Center for the Study of Learning, Georgetown University Medical Center, Washington, DC
statistical_approach: Whole-brain voxel-based morphometry (VBM); ABCD Study N=1,209 Spanish-English bilingual vs. English monolingual children matched on age, sex, SES, and nonverbal reasoning
abstract: Using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, this study compared neuroanatomy between large groups of early cultural Spanish-English bilingual and English monolingual children (N=1,209) matched on age, pubertal status, sex, handedness, socioeconomic status, and nonverbal reasoning. Whole-brain voxel-based morphometry revealed areas of greater and lesser gray matter volume in bilinguals than monolinguals across all lobes, contributing new insight into the neuroanatomical correlates of early childhood bilingualism.

title: Linking the exposome to the brain-behaviour phenotype
authors: Sarah Genon, Agustin Ibanez, Masoud Tahmasian, et al.
institution: Institute of Neuroscience and Medicine, Forschungszentrum Jülich, Germany
statistical_approach: Narrative synthesis; exposome concept framework; review of interindividual variability in brain-behaviour phenotype across the lifespan
abstract: This Perspective examines how environmental, lifestyle, and biological exposures across the lifespan — varying in timing, duration, and intensity — interact with genetic factors to shape an individual's neurocognitive phenotype. The exposome concept offers a valuable framework to better understand interindividual variability in the brain-behaviour phenotype as well as vulnerability and resilience to brain diseases. The authors discuss methodological advances, challenges, and opportunities for linking the totality of non-genetic exposures to brain structure, function, and behaviour across development.

title: Where is ADHD in the brain? Evidence for a neurodevelopmental continuum of brain dynamics
authors: Gian Marco Duma, Giulia Stefanelli, Lisa Toffoli, Giulia Ferri, Giovanni Pellegrino, Alberto Danieli, Federica Martinez, Vincenza Tarantino, Duncan Astle, Fiorella Del Popolo Cristaldi, Giovanni Mento
institution: IRCCS Eugenio Medea, Bosisio Parini, Italy
statistical_approach: High-density EEG (hdEEG); brain fluidity and region-specific cortical excitability metrics; clustering analysis; N=84 (51 ADHD, 33 typically developing), ages 8-17
abstract: This study tested whether ADHD and typically developing peers differ in neural dynamics and cortical excitability, and whether brain-behavior relationships align with diagnostic or developmental dimensions. Using high-density EEG in 84 participants aged 8-17 (51 ADHD, 33 typically developing), the study measured brain fluidity and region-specific cortical excitability from task-free data. While ADHD is associated with mean-level differences in neural dynamics, brain-behavior organization follows a developmental neurocognitive-affective axis that transcends the diagnostic boundary. Clustering revealed two neurodevelopmental profiles spanning both groups, challenging categorical diagnostic models of ADHD.

title: Parent-mediated early intervention in infants and toddlers at elevated likelihood for autism: a systematic review of randomized controlled trials
authors: Conti E, Ieri F, Calderoni S, Apicella F, Chericoni N, Costanzo V, Marchi V, Guzzetta A, Colombi C
institution: University of Pisa, Department of Clinical and Experimental Medicine, Pisa, Italy
statistical_approach: Systematic review (PRISMA) of randomized controlled trials of parent-mediated early interventions for infants/toddlers at elevated likelihood for ASD
abstract: While prodromal signs of ASD can be observed during the first months of life, most care approaches require a formal diagnosis before children can receive autism-specialized intervention services. This systematic review of randomized controlled trials synthesizes evidence on parent-mediated interventions for infants and toddlers at elevated likelihood for ASD, examining efficacy, mechanisms, and implications for early brain development and behavioral trajectories. Current prevalence of autism spectrum disorder has risen to 1 in 31 according to recent CDC estimates, increasing urgency for early detection and intervention approaches.

title: A shift in brain functional systems during mother-child neural synchrony marks children's cognitive development
authors: Hui Zhao, Yu Zhai, Xiangyu He, Zhengyan Wang, Chunming Lu
institution: Beijing Normal University, China
statistical_approach: fNIRS hyperscanning, wavelet coherence, inter-neural synchrony (INS), mother-child dyad collaborative task
abstract: Using fNIRS hyperscanning in mother-child dyads, this study found that the brain functional systems underlying inter-neural synchrony shift across preschool development. INS in TPJ-TPJ connections supported mutual responsiveness, while TPJ-IFC INS supported children's responsiveness to caregivers, with these patterns differing across ages. INS mediated the contribution of mother-child interaction quality to children's self-regulation and cognitive development, suggesting social neurophysiology of parent-child interaction bridges caregiving quality and cognitive outcomes.

title: Dysfunctional brain connectivity in late preterm infants: cerebral networks and implications for learning
authors: Maria I. Herrera et al.
institution: National University of the South / INBIOSUR-CONICET, Argentina
statistical_approach: Narrative review; synthesis of cerebral connectivity and learning outcomes literature in late preterm infants
abstract: This review examines the neurobiological basis of dysfunctional brain connectivity in late preterm infants (34-36 gestational weeks) and its consequences for learning and cognitive development. Late preterm birth disrupts the maturation of cerebral networks during a period of rapid synaptogenesis and myelination, with downstream effects on functional connectivity architectures. The authors review structural, functional, and electrophysiological evidence linking preterm-specific connectivity alterations to learning and cognitive trajectories, and discuss implications for early identification and intervention strategies.

title: Single-cell multiomic analysis of the developing Down syndrome neocortex reveals altered neuron type composition
authors: Vuong CK, Weber A, Seong P, Chen YJ, Peyer J, Younesi S, Salinda A, Gomez D, Rivas G, Morales A, Shafie B, Zhang P, Nichterwitz S, Qi L, Fernandez NT, Friedman E, Love MI, Gandal MJ, Geschwind DH, Lowry WE, Stein JL, de la Torre-Ubieta L
institution: David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA
statistical_approach: Single-nucleus RNA-seq and ATAC-seq, >100,000 nuclei from 26 donors at gestational weeks 13-23, cell type composition analysis, transcription factor regulatory network analysis
abstract: Using single-nucleus multiomic sequencing of >100,000 nuclei from 26 Down syndrome and control donors at gestational weeks 13-23, this study found that progenitor cells in trisomy 21 undergo an accelerated transition to neurogenesis, skewing the balance of excitatory neuron types in the developing neocortex. The premature shift from stem cell maintenance to neuron production alters the proportion of RORB- and FOXP1-expressing neuron subtypes. Transcription factor analysis identified BACH1, PKNOX1, and GABPA—all encoded on chromosome 21—as central regulatory hubs driving the altered cell composition.

title: Caregiver-infant interactions selectively shape emerging functional connectivity in the neonatal brain
authors: L. Carnevali, B. Blanco, M. Rozhko, M. Weatherhead, M. H. Johnson, S. Lloyd-Fox
institution: Birkbeck, University of London
statistical_approach: Functional connectivity analysis, linear mixed models, dyadic interaction coding
abstract: This study combined observations of caregiver-infant interactions at one month with repeated home-based brain imaging using fNIRS. Variations in dyadic engagement and affectionate touch influenced the organization and development of infant brain functional connectivity. Affectionate caregiver-infant interactions were associated with stronger functional connectivity, particularly within the social brain networks.

title: Trajectories of hippocampal subregion development in the first years of life predict school-age episodic memory
authors: Stoyell SM, Lundquist JT, Hantzsch L, Bunnell A, Thomas KM, Fair DA, Tervo-Clemmens B, Feczko E, Elison JT
institution: University of Minnesota / Masonic Institute for the Developing Brain
statistical_approach: Linear mixed effects models, 835 MRI visits from 322 participants (Baby Connectome Project), hippocampal subregion volumetry (head/body/tail), predictive modeling for school-age episodic memory
abstract: Analyzing 835 longitudinal MRI visits from 322 participants (ages 0-4 years) in the Baby Connectome Project, this study tracked volumetric development of hippocampal head, body, and tail subregions across early life. Hippocampal head showed a steeper volumetric growth plateau relative to body and tail, indicating differential maturation trajectories across subregions. Early hippocampal subregion volume trajectories predicted school-age episodic memory outcomes, establishing a link between early hippocampal architecture and later cognitive function.

title: Cortical reconstruction and anatomical parcellation of high-resolution multi-modal postmortem ex vivo MRI of the human infant brain
authors: Pulkit Khandelwal, Sala Young, Nathan Xi Ngo, Paul A. Yushkevich, Andre van der Kouwe, Robin L. Haynes, Hannah C. Kinney, Lilla Zöllei
institution: Massachusetts General Hospital / Harvard Medical School
statistical_approach: Deep learning volumetric segmentation, PostSynth synthetic data generation, cortical surface reconstruction
abstract: This study introduces a multi-modal high-resolution postmortem infant MRI dataset and unified computational framework combining deep learning volumetric segmentation with surface-based cortical reconstruction. PostSynth, a postmortem-specific synthetic data generation engine, explicitly models fixation-driven imaging characteristics including altered gray-white matter contrast, laminar cortical intensity heterogeneity, and specimen-specific bias fields. The framework enables automated anatomical segmentation and cortical surface reconstruction in postmortem infant brains.

title: Representational similarity of hemodynamic brain responses to written and spoken words increases when learning to read
authors: Kazuki Maruo, Roman Kessler, et al.
institution: Kiel University / Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences
statistical_approach: Representational similarity analysis (RSA), fMRI BOLD, 61 primary school children varying in reading experience and school grade
abstract: This fMRI study examined how reading experience shapes audiovisual brain representations of written and spoken words in 61 primary school children. Using representational similarity analysis, audiovisual similarity of activation patterns in left inferior frontal gyrus, inferior parietal lobule, superior temporal gyrus, and temporo-occipital cortex increased linearly with school grade, with the largest effect in the left posterior superior temporal gyrus. Learning to read drives progressive integration of visual word form and speech representations within canonical language areas.

title: Multimodal Assessment of Cerebral Perfusion and EEG Maturation in Preterm Infants at Term-Equivalent Age
authors: [Authors] et al.
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Ultra-micro angiography (UMA) for cerebral microperfusion, amplitude-integrated EEG (aEEG) functional scores, group comparison across 76 neonates (23 extremely/very preterm, 27 moderate-late preterm, 26 term controls)
abstract: This prospective study evaluated 76 neonates—23 extremely/very preterm, 27 moderate-late preterm, and 26 full-term controls—using combined ultra-micro angiography (UMA) for cerebral microperfusion and amplitude-integrated EEG (aEEG) for brain function assessment at term-equivalent age. Both cerebral perfusion and EEG maturation showed progressive improvements with increasing gestational age at birth. The multimodal combination of UMA perfusion imaging and aEEG provides complementary insight into preterm brain development and may identify infants at risk for neurodevelopmental delay.

title: Neonatal Resting-State Functional Connectivity Predicts Socioemotional and Behavioral Outcomes at 18 Months
authors: Mi Zou, Arun L. W. Bokde
institution: Trinity College Dublin
statistical_approach: Stability-driven ROI-constrained connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM), Developing Human Connectome Project
abstract: This study examined whether neonatal functional connectivity predicts 18-month behavioral and temperament outcomes in 397 infants (277 term-born, 120 preterm-born) using resting-state fMRI data from the Developing Human Connectome Project. Outcomes were assessed using the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) and Early Childhood Behavior Questionnaire (ECBQ). Significant predictive models were found for externalizing, internalizing, surgency, negative affect, and effortful control, with clear differences in predictive architecture between term-born and preterm-born groups. Prefrontal and temporoparietal systems were repeatedly implicated.

title: Explicitly nonlinear fMRI networks reveal hidden trajectories of infant brain development
authors: Spencer Kinsey, Geethanjali Nagaboina, Prerana Bajracharya, Masoud Seraji, Zening Fu, Vince D. Calhoun, Sarah Shultz, Armin Iraji
institution: Georgia State University / Emory University / TReNDS Center
statistical_approach: Data-driven nonlinear ICA, intrinsic connectivity networks, developmental regression
abstract: This study presents the first comprehensive developmental investigation of whole-brain nonlinear fMRI networks in human infants. A data-driven approach captured brain intrinsic connectivity networks from explicitly nonlinear resting-state fMRI connectivity, profiling their developmental associations in typically developing infants. Neurobiologically structured nonlinear fMRI connectivity patterns were identified during early postnatal life, indicating macroscale brain ensembles systematically participate in nonlinear relationships at birth. Linear and nonlinear network counterparts are linked to partially overlapping but complementary developmental profiles, with the nonlinear approach unveiling insights into sensorimotor, default mode, executive functioning, language production, and stimulus saliency networks.

title: Instability of Alpha Oscillatory States in Autism and Familial Liability: Evidence from Burst-Resolved High-Density Electroencephalography (EEG)
authors: Theo Vanneau, Chloe Brittenham, Megan Darrell, Michael Quiquempoix, John J. Foxe, Sophie Molholm
institution: Albert Einstein College of Medicine, New York
statistical_approach: Burst-resolved alpha power analysis, time-frequency decomposition, group comparison (non-autistic n=39, autistic n=52, siblings n=26)
abstract: This study investigated the temporal dynamics of alpha oscillations (7-13 Hz) in autistic children (n=52), non-autistic children (n=39), and siblings of autistic children (n=26) aged 8-14 years using burst-resolved high-density EEG. Reduced alpha power in autism was found to reflect reduced temporal stability of alpha bursts rather than reduced burst amplitude. Familial liability was associated with intermediate burst stability between autistic and non-autistic groups, suggesting alpha burst dynamics as a biologically meaningful endophenotype of autism.

title: Separable Brain Maturation Patterns Mediate Exposome Influences on Cognitive Development: A Longitudinal Study
authors: Jiadong Yan, Judy Chen, Bin Wan, Paule Joanne Toussaint, Alan C. Evans, Sherif Karama
institution: McGill University
statistical_approach: Mediation analysis, factor analysis, 4,882 multimodal brain features, 112 exposome variables across six domains
abstract: This longitudinal study examined whether brain maturation mediates the relationship between the exposome and general cognitive ability (g) in a large longitudinal cohort (N=1,112, ages 9-15), using 4,882 multimodal brain features and 112 exposome variables spanning six domains. Factor analysis of domain-level mediation patterns revealed four latent factors, each associated with a largely distinct neural substrate, suggesting that different exposome domains influence cognitive development through partially separable brain mechanisms. The family environment domain showed the strongest correspondence with brain maturation patterns.

title: Neonatal sensory networks at birth predict cognitive, language, and motor outcomes at 18 months
authors: Mi Zou, Arun L. W. Bokde
institution: Trinity College Dublin
statistical_approach: ROI-degree-guided connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM), Developing Human Connectome Project
abstract: Using data from 402 infants (278 term-born, 124 preterm-born) in the Developing Human Connectome Project, this study introduced an ROI-constrained variant of Connectome-Based Predictive Modeling (CPM) that incorporates ROI-degree-guided feature selection. Models robustly predicted Bayley-III cognitive, language, and motor outcomes at 18 months. Dominant predictive features in term-born infants concentrated in visual-auditory interactions and connections between sensory networks and other cortical regions, identifying early sensory hubs as biomarkers for later neurodevelopmental outcomes.

title: Qualitative EEG abnormalities in autism spectrum disorder reflect inhibition-dominated brain dynamics indexed by the functional E/I ratio
authors: Avramiea AE, Juarez-Martinez EL, Garcés P, et al.
institution: Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam / EU-AIMS Longitudinal European Autism Project
statistical_approach: Functional E/I ratio (fE/I) derived from alpha oscillations, EU-AIMS dataset (267 ASD, 209 controls), group comparison with clinical EEG abnormality stratification
abstract: Using resting-state EEG from 267 autistic individuals and 209 controls in the EU-AIMS Longitudinal European Autism Project, this study examined the functional excitation/inhibition (E/I) ratio derived from alpha oscillations. Children and adolescents with autism displaying qualitative EEG abnormalities showed significantly lower alpha power and reduced fE/I, indicating inhibition-dominated brain dynamics. This finding bridges qualitative clinical EEG assessment with quantitative E/I balance markers, suggesting alpha-band fE/I captures a neurophysiological subtype within the autism spectrum.

title: Neural correlates of joint attention in infants aged 8-24 months: a systematic review of EEG, fNIRS, and fMRI studies
authors: Vera Mateus, Ana Carolina Santos, Ana Ganho-Ávila, Mónica Sobral, Ana Osório, Sara Cruz
institution: University of Coimbra, Portugal
statistical_approach: Systematic review (PRISMA), 16 studies: EEG (n=11), fNIRS (n=3), fMRI (n=2); search updated May 2025
abstract: This systematic review examined neural correlates of joint attention in 16 studies of typically developing infants aged 8-24 months using EEG (n=11), fNIRS (n=3), and fMRI (n=2). EEG studies identified alpha-band desynchronization and enhanced negative central (Nc) components in frontal, central, and parietal regions during joint attention paradigms. fNIRS studies found increased activation in superior temporal sulcus-temporoparietal junction and prefrontal regions. fMRI revealed associations between joint attention initiation and functional connectivity in visual, dorsal attention, and default mode networks. These findings illuminate the distributed neural architecture supporting early joint attention development.

title: Developmental links between play behavior and brain network integration across infancy to middle childhood
authors: Authors from multiple institutions (bioRxiv)
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Resting-state fMRI network analysis, functional connectivity, between-network coupling
abstract: Three studies examined the relationship between play behavior and fMRI-based functional connectivity. Study 1 (N=143, 10 months-3 years): play and imitation behaviors were associated with stronger within-DMN connectivity and DMN-cognitive control network coupling. Study 2 (N=108, 4-11 years): children with more frequent play showed higher DMN-salience and cognitive control-salience connectivity. Study 3 (N=45, 4-12 years): Montessori-educated children showed higher DMN-salience and DMN-cognitive control connectivity vs. traditional schooling. Consistent finding: play is associated with greater default mode, salience, and cognitive control network integration.

title: Cascading periods of language-related brain plasticity across early childhood
authors: Monica E. Ellwood-Lowe, Monami Nishio, Alexander J. Dufford, Michael Arcaro, Theodore D. Satterthwaite, Allyson P. Mackey
institution: University of Pennsylvania
statistical_approach: Hurst exponent analysis, Baby Connectome Project (n=222, 458 observations), HCP-Development (n=324), growth curve modeling
abstract: Using two large developmental datasets spanning 10 months to 15 years (Baby Connectome Project and HCP-Development), this study characterized Hurst exponent development in language-related brain regions. Hurst exponent—a measure of neural excitability and plasticity—increases in a graded fashion, with posterior regions developing earlier than anterior regions. Thalamic Hurst plateaus in the first year of life, while cortical Hurst plateaus around age 9. Children with higher language skills showed slower Hurst increases in early childhood, suggesting protracted cortical plasticity supports better language development.

title: Functional connectome harmonics capture early brain organization and maturity in neonates
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Functional connectome harmonics (FCH), entropy/power/energy metrics, dHCP dataset (714 neonates)
abstract: This study applied functional connectome harmonics (FCH) to resting-state fMRI data from 714 neonates in the Developing Human Connectome Project, mapping early brain organization and maturity. FCH-derived metrics—entropy, power, and energy—were computed from neonatal functional connectivity. Power and energy were higher in term-born compared to preterm neonates; entropy was elevated in preterms. FCH metrics predicted approximately 30% of post-menstrual age variance, suggesting their utility as early markers of brain maturity and prematurity biomarkers.

title: Spectral Geometry of Infant Resting-State fNIRS Connectivity: Bilingual vs Monolingual
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Spectral geometry on SPD correlation matrices, JBLD barycentric mean, Grassmann manifold features, leave-one-out validation (n=94)
abstract: This study examined whether bilingual versus monolingual language environments in early infancy are associated with differences in intrinsic functional organization measured from resting-state fNIRS. Using the RS4 infant resting-state fNIRS cohort (N=94), shrinkage-regularized SPD correlation operators and spectral geometry methods were applied. Correlation-based Grassmannian features achieved consistent above-chance separability (ROC-AUC=0.811), with late fusion improving to 0.838 and cross-fusion yielding ROC-AUC of 0.871, suggesting that bilingual exposure induces detectable differences in infant brain network organization.

title: Resting-state periodic and aperiodic brain oscillations from birth to preschool years: Aperiodic maturity predicts developmental course
authors: Heather L Green, James Christopher Edgar, Kylie Mol, Marybeth McNamee, Laura Prosser, Mina Kim, Emily S Kuschner, Gregory A Miller, Yuhan Chen
institution: Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, Department of Radiology
statistical_approach: Specparam (FOOOF), power spectrum analysis, nonlinear age modeling, mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study characterizes the developmental trajectory of resting-state periodic and aperiodic brain oscillations from birth through the preschool years using MEG. Applying the Specparam (FOOOF) algorithm, the authors separately model aperiodic (1/f) and periodic (oscillatory) components of the power spectrum across a broad age range (2–68 months). Findings show that aperiodic maturity — the degree to which the aperiodic exponent approaches adult-like values — predicts subsequent developmental course, suggesting it as a biomarker of neural maturation.
key_figure: Aperiodic exponent developmental trajectories from birth to preschool with age-nonlinear curve fits

title: Neurodevelopmental disorders in children: the role of advanced MRI in early detection and biomarker identification
authors: [Authors] et al.
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Systematic narrative review; advanced MRI techniques including arterial spin labeling (ASL), quantitative MRI (qMRI), fNIRS integration
abstract: This review surveys advanced MRI-based neuroimaging techniques for early detection of neurodevelopmental disorders in children, covering arterial spin labeling (ASL), quantitative MRI (qMRI), and integration with fNIRS. The paper discusses current MRI-derived biomarkers for autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, cerebral palsy, and related conditions across infancy and early childhood. Translational challenges and future directions for clinical implementation of advanced neuroimaging for early diagnosis of neurodevelopmental disorders are highlighted.

title: Dynamics of the attentional blink in preverbal infants
authors: F. Leroy, S. Naik, R. Gulbinaite, M. Palu, D. Battaglia, G. Dehaene-Lambertz
institution: CEA (Commissariat à l'énergie atomique et aux énergies alternatives), France
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
statistical_approach: Saccade analysis, pupillometry, steady-state EEG frequency-tagging, temporal dynamics modeling
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study examines whether preverbal 4-month-old infants exhibit the attentional blink — a temporal limitation in visual attention where a second target presented shortly after a first target is often missed. Using combined EEG and eye-tracking methods with steady-state visual evoked potential paradigms, the authors demonstrate that infants show attention dynamics analogous to the attentional blink seen in adults, revealing early-emerging constraints on temporal attention in the developing brain.
key_figure: Attentional blink curves (detection accuracy vs. inter-target lag) in 4-month-old infants compared to adult benchmarks

title: Visual Cortical Response Variability in Infants at High Familial Likelihood for Autism
authors: Abigail Dickinson, Madison Booth, Scott Huberty, Declan Ryan, Alana Campbell, Jessica B. Girault, Neely Miller, Bonnie Lau, John Zempel, Sara Jane Webb, Jed Elison, Adrian K.C. Lee, Annette Estes, Stephen R. Dager, Heather Hazlett, Jason Wolff, Robert Schultz, Natasha Marrus, Alan Evans, Joseph Piven, John R. Pruett Jr., Shafali Jeste
institution: IBIS Network (multiple institutions)
statistical_approach: Visual evoked potential (VEP) P1 latency variability, group comparison, regression on Bayley-III outcomes at 24 months
abstract: Pattern-reversal visual evoked potentials (VEPs) at 6 and 12 months were measured in 177/132 infants with high familial likelihood for autism (IBIS-EP study). P1 latency variability—trial-to-trial consistency—was significantly associated with cognitive and language skills at 24 months. Greater trial-to-trial variability in P1 latency at both timepoints predicted higher cognitive and language scores. Temporal variability in visual cortical response timing emerged as the strongest neural correlate of later developmental outcomes, suggesting adaptive sensory circuit flexibility facilitates experience-dependent tuning.

title: The emergence of the language system in the toddler brain
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Univariate fMRI contrasts, left vs. right laterality analysis, Sesame Street audio paradigm (forward vs. reversed)
abstract: Awake fMRI data from 29 toddlers aged 19-36 months during a child-friendly language task (forward vs. backward Sesame Street audio). Left frontal and temporal cortex already responded more to comprehensible language than reversed audio. Although response magnitude was weaker than in adults, brain organization was already adult-like in its left-hemispheric lateralization, challenging claims that frontal language processing does not emerge until later in life. The findings demonstrate that the language system is established in its functional architecture by 19-36 months.

title: Wired from the womb: a narrative review of fetal and neonatal brain connectomics via functional MRI
authors: Shukla A, Chowdhary V, Hall RW
institution: University of Arkansas for Medical Sciences
statistical_approach: Narrative review synthesis; resting-state fMRI, ICA, network modeling; synthesizes 2007-2025 fMRI connectomics research in fetuses and neonates
abstract: This narrative review synthesizes nearly two decades of research (2007-2025) applying functional MRI to map fetal and neonatal brain connectomes. Key topics include resting-state fMRI methodology in utero and postnatally, independent component analysis of large-scale functional networks, and normative developmental modeling. The review highlights how functional network architecture emerges prenatally and is refined during the neonatal period, with emphasis on default mode network development, sensorimotor network emergence, and clinical applications for preterm brain assessment.

title: Neonatal sleep physiology and polysomnographic indices predict executive functioning at age 2 in preterm children
authors: Dereymaeker A, Hermans T, Bollen B, et al. (Neonatal Sleep Talks NEST group)
institution: University Hospitals Leuven, Belgium
age_range: Preterm neonates to 2 years corrected age
statistical_approach: Overnight polysomnography metrics, linear regression, 76 preterm infants (<34 weeks and/or <1500g), Bayley-III executive function at 2 years corrected age
abstract: In 76 preterm infants born below 34 weeks gestational age and/or 1500g, overnight polysomnography was recorded at term-equivalent age. Total sleep time and specific sleep architecture indices from polysomnography predicted higher executive functioning scores on the Bayley-III at 2 years corrected age. These findings suggest that neonatal sleep physiology is a functional window into maturing neural circuits supporting executive function, offering potential biomarkers for early identification of neurodevelopmental risk in preterm populations.

title: Sleep and infant development in the first year: neurobiological underpinnings and longitudinal correlates
authors: O'Connor C, Ventura S, Proietti J, et al.
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Narrative review synthesis; longitudinal studies with polysomnography, actigraphy, and parental report across the first year
abstract: This review synthesizes evidence on the relationship between sleep and neurodevelopment across the first year of life. Sleep architecture undergoes fundamental changes during infancy, with the dominant sleep state shifting from active (REM-like) sleep in neonates to NREM-predominant sleep by 12 months, mirroring cortical maturation. These transitions predict developmental outcomes in motor, cognitive, and socioemotional domains. The review discusses neurobiological mechanisms linking sleep to synaptic consolidation and network maturation in infancy, with clinical implications for early sleep assessment.

title: Tracing infant sleep neurophysiology longitudinally from 3 to 6 months: EEG insights into brain development
authors: Matthieu Beaugrand, Valeria Jaramillo, Christophe Mühlematter, Sarah F. Schoch, Vivien Reicher, Andjela Markovic, Salome Kurth
institution: University of Surrey, Surrey Sleep Research Centre
statistical_approach: EEG power spectral analysis, topographic mapping, longitudinal mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This longitudinal study traces changes in infant sleep neurophysiology using EEG from 3 to 6 months of age. By analyzing EEG power across frequency bands during sleep, the authors characterize developmental changes in sleep architecture and brain oscillatory activity across this key window of early brain development, providing normative benchmarks for sleep EEG maturation.
key_figure: Topographic maps of EEG power across sleep stages at 3 and 6 months

title: Functional connectivity in infants' visual cortex and its links to motion processing and autism
authors: Irzam Hardiansyah, Giorgia Bussu, Sven Bölte, Emily J. H. Jones, Terje Falck-Ytter
institution: Karolinska Institutet / Uppsala University
statistical_approach: EEG functional connectivity (theta, alpha, gamma), social scene viewing paradigm, EASE cohort (N=59, 39 elevated familial likelihood for autism)
abstract: This study assessed 5-month-old infants (n=59; 39 with elevated familial likelihood of autism) with EEG while they watched videos showing social and non-social scenes from the Early Autism in Sweden (EASE) cohort. Gamma connectivity between midline and far-lateral visual cortex during social scene viewing was linked to both later autism symptoms and global motion visual cortical laterality. The results suggest that altered early visual cortical functional connectivity may represent an EEG biomarker linking visual motion processing atypicalities to later autism symptomatology.

title: From Variability to Synchrony: Non-linear Development of Auditory Neural Responses During the First Year of Life
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Inter-trial phase coherence (ITPC), high-density EEG, longitudinal mixed models (2 weeks, 6 months, 12 months, N=69)
abstract: This longitudinal high-density EEG study followed 69 infants at 2 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months using pure tone auditory stimuli. Neural responses became more pronounced with age and showed reduced trial-to-trial variability. Phase synchronization increased from 2 weeks to 6 months in a broad low-frequency range (0-11 Hz). Unexpectedly, synchronization decreased from 6 to 12 months, suggesting a developmental transition toward more differentiated brain activity. These non-linear trajectories indicate dynamic shifts in neural synchronization across the first year of life.

title: Regional BOLD variability reflects microstructural maturation and neuronal ensheathment in the preterm infant cortex
authors: Sa de Almeida, J., Boehringer, A., Loukas, S., and co-authors
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Longitudinal rs-fMRI BOLD variability analysis, multi-shell diffusion MRI, gene expression analysis, N=54 preterm infants + 24 full-term
abstract: This longitudinal study combined rs-fMRI and multi-shell diffusion imaging in 54 very preterm infants (at 33 weeks GA and term-equivalent age) and 24 full-term newborns. During preterm development, BOLD variability increased in primary sensory-sensorimotor and proto-DMN regions, accompanied by decreased cortical diffusivity. Gene expression analysis revealed concurrent upregulation of gliogenesis and neuronal ensheathment genes. By term age, preterm infants showed immature cortical maturation patterns compared with full-term newborns, linking BOLD variability to ongoing microstructural maturation.

title: The Fronto-Temporal Cortex Has Increased Subcortical Connectivity In Utero and Plasticity in Adulthood
authors: Gabriela Epihova, Dimitar Z. Epihov, Danyal Akarca, Duncan E. Astle
institution: MRC Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit, University of Cambridge
statistical_approach: Fetal MRI connectivity analysis, gene expression annotation, perineuronal net markers, myelination quantification
abstract: At 24 postgestational weeks, fronto-temporal regions are disproportionately connected to subcortical regions, highlighting their role in early integrative cortical-subcortical communication. In adulthood, fronto-temporal cortex shows lower myelin content and higher expression of undifferentiated progenitor cell markers, as well as lower expression of perineuronal net marker genes, suggesting a heightened state of plasticity. This finding suggests fronto-temporal regions evolved extended developmental timelines to maximize flexible neural responses during postnatal experience.

title: Developmental and aging trajectories of 40-Hz auditory steady-state responses: a systematic review across the human lifespan
authors: Aurimas Mockevičius, Danylo Machevskyi, Dariusz Majcherczyk, Inga Griškova-Bulanova
institution: Life Sciences Center, Vilnius University, Lithuania
statistical_approach: Systematic review (PRISMA), meta-analysis of 40-Hz ASSR developmental trajectories across the lifespan
abstract: This systematic review examined developmental and aging trajectories of 40-Hz auditory steady-state responses (ASSRs), sensitive markers of temporal precision, excitation-inhibition (E/I) balance, and functional connectivity in the auditory cortex. ASSRs show pronounced maturation from infancy through adolescence, reflecting E/I balance shifts associated with GABAergic circuit development, and decline with aging. The 40-Hz ASSR is positioned as a translational biomarker with applications in neurodevelopmental disorders including autism and schizophrenia.

title: Explainable AI uncovers novel EEG microstate candidate neurophysiological markers for autism spectrum disorder
authors: Delna Kuriyakose, Gowsalya M.
institution: Vellore Institute of Technology, India
statistical_approach: EEG microstates, XGBoost classifier (80.87% accuracy), SHAP explainability, multi-domain feature extraction (temporal, spectral, complexity, graph-theoretic)
abstract: This study proposes a novel multi-domain analytical pipeline extracting and integrating EEG microstate features from four domains: temporal dynamics (microstate duration, transition entropy), spectral power within microstate-defined segments, complexity metrics (permutation entropy, fractal dimension), and graph-theoretical measures from microstate transition matrices. XGBoost achieved 80.87% classification accuracy for ASD vs. neurotypical. SHAP-based interpretability identified fractional occupancy derivative for microstate 3, delta-band power, and mean inter-transition interval as top discriminative features, providing interpretable neurophysiological biomarkers for ASD.

title: Unpredictable caregiving is associated with disrupted neurophysiological measures of attention and autonomic function in three-month-old infants
authors: Annie Brandes-Aitken, Maya Metser, Stephen H. Braren, Sarah C. Vogel, Natalie H. Brito
institution: New York University, Department of Applied Psychology
statistical_approach: Frontal theta power analysis, heart rate variability (HRV) analysis, regression models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study examines how unpredictable caregiving environments are associated with neurophysiological markers of attention and autonomic regulation in 3-month-old infants. EEG measures of frontal theta power and heart rate variability (HRV) are used to index attentional and autonomic function. Results indicate that infants exposed to more unpredictable caregiving show disrupted patterns of neural attention and autonomic regulation, highlighting early neural consequences of caregiving adversity.
key_figure: Correlation between caregiving unpredictability scores and infant frontal theta power/HRV metrics

title: Machine learning and deep learning applied to EEG and fNIRS for early autism spectrum disorder diagnosis: a systematic review
authors: De Giacomo A, Palmieri R, Russo EF, Pizzolorusso I, Brandi R, Magistro F, Di Cesare MG, Quattrocelli S, Cardone D, Perpetuini D, Merla A
institution: DiBraiN University Hospital Bari / University G. d'Annunzio Chieti-Pescara, Italy
statistical_approach: Systematic review (PRISMA), 27 studies from 2019-2024, ML/DL classification metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC)
abstract: This systematic review synthesized 27 peer-reviewed studies (2019-2024) investigating machine learning and deep learning applied to EEG and fNIRS signals for early autism spectrum disorder diagnosis. Deep learning architectures—particularly convolutional neural networks and recurrent models—showed strong performance for ASD classification. Best results were obtained with paradigms targeting social perception and resting-state connectivity. Key challenges include small sample sizes, limited cross-dataset generalizability, and lack of standardized protocols for early-age recordings.

title: Infants have rich visual categories in ventrotemporal cortex at 2 months of age
authors: Cliona O'Doherty, Áine T. Dineen, Anna Truzzi, Graham King, Lorijn Zaadnoordijk, Keelin Harrison, Enna-Louise D'Arcy, Jessica White, Chiara Caldinelli, Tamrin Holloway, Anna Kravchenko, Jörn Diedrichsen, Ailbhe Tarrant, Angela T. Byrne, Adrienne Foran, Eleanor J. Molloy, Rhodri Cusack
institution: Trinity College Dublin
statistical_approach: Representational similarity analysis (RSA), deep neural network comparison, multivariate pattern analysis
abstract: This awake fMRI study of over 100 2-month-old infants (with follow-up at 9 months) found that categorical structure is present in high-level visual cortex from 2 months of age. Infants viewed images from 12 familiar visual categories while brain activity was recorded. Category representations in ventrotemporal cortex were similar to adult representations, including animate/inanimate distinctions. A deep neural network model aligned with infants' representational geometry, indicating early category templates can be learned from statistics of visual input. Categorical structure precedes its emergence in lateral visual cortex, suggesting non-hierarchical development.

title: EEG trajectories across the first two years of life are associated with maternal depression, anxiety, and perceived stress
authors: Viviane Valdes, Jennifer Near, Emily K Walsh, Lara Pierce, Kathleen Conroy, Lianna R. Lipton, Mei A. Elansary, Charles A. Nelson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
statistical_approach: Linear mixed models, EEG power spectral analysis, 211 families followed longitudinally with EEG at 2, 6, 9, 12, and 24 months
abstract: This longitudinal study followed 211 families for two years, measuring infant resting-state EEG at 2, 6, 9, 12, and 24 months of age. Higher maternal depression, anxiety, and perceived stress were associated with lower infant high-frequency EEG power (beta and gamma bands) across the first two years of life, independent of socioeconomic status and infant temperament. Findings suggest that maternal mental health shapes early neural oscillatory development, with particular sensitivity in higher-frequency bands linked to cortical maturation and inhibitory function.

title: Single-cell atlas of the developing Down syndrome fetal cortex reveals altered excitatory neuron composition
authors: Lattke M, Tan WL, Sukumaran SK, Utami KH, Sintes M, Sakthivel S, Tan J, Lim A, Bansal VA, Rekopoulou K, Matthews N, Alić I, Krsnik Ž, Nižetić D, Levi BP, De Paola V
institution: Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore
statistical_approach: Single-cell RNA sequencing, ~250,000 cells from 15 Down syndrome and 15 control fetal cortices (10-20 weeks postconception), UMAP clustering, transcription factor regulatory network analysis
abstract: Profiling ~250,000 cells from 15 Down syndrome and 15 control fetal cortices at 10-20 weeks postconception, this study constructed the first single-cell atlas of the developing Down syndrome cortex. RORB- and FOXP1-expressing excitatory neuron subtypes were significantly reduced in Down syndrome relative to controls. Transcription factor hub analysis identified BACH1, PKNOX1, and GABPA—all encoded on chromosome 21—as central regulators of the altered cell type composition. These findings link trisomy 21-encoded genes to specific changes in excitatory neuron identity during early cortical development.

title: Effects of Early Infant Nutrition on Aperiodic Exponent From 2 to 12 Months Using EEG Analysis
authors: Dylan Gilbreath, Adam Andrews, Darcy Hagood, Aline Andres, Linda J Larson-Prior
institution: University of Arkansas for Medical Sciences
statistical_approach: Power spectral density (Specparam/FOOOF), mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study examines how early infant nutrition (breastfeeding vs. formula) influences the EEG aperiodic exponent (1/f slope) from 2 to 12 months of age. Using power spectral density analysis with the Specparam toolbox, the authors track longitudinal changes in aperiodic EEG activity, a marker of excitation/inhibition balance. Results indicate nutrition-related differences in the developmental trajectory of the aperiodic exponent across early infancy.
key_figure: Longitudinal aperiodic exponent trajectories by feeding group across 2, 6, and 12-month timepoints

title: Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging in Awake Infants: Insights From More Than 750 Scanning Sessions
authors: Behm et al.
institution: Yale University / MIT
statistical_approach: Descriptive analysis of 766 fMRI sessions in awake infants (1-36 months) from two labs, logistic/polynomial regression for session success predictors, data quality metrics
abstract: Reporting on 766 fMRI sessions in awake infants aged 1-36 months collected across two labs (Yale and MIT), this study documents practical and methodological insights for awake infant fMRI research. Younger infants more often successfully entered the scanner, while 12-24 month-old infants showed higher functional data retention after preprocessing than younger and older infants. The paper provides evidence-based recommendations for optimizing awake infant fMRI protocols and highlights implications for the growing renaissance of task-based fMRI studies in this age group.

title: Neonatal brain-age models in full- and preterm infants
authors: Howard Chiu, Adam Richie-Halford, Molly F. Lazarus, et al.
institution: University of Washington
statistical_approach: Diffusion MRI (dMRI) brain-age modeling, cross-validation across two datasets, brain-age gap analysis, bootstrap confidence intervals
abstract: This study developed brain-age prediction models using diffusion MRI (dMRI) data in neonates from two independent datasets. Models of neonatal dMRI data predicted post-menstrual age within days in full-term infants and within approximately one week in preterm infants. Brain-age gap (predicted minus actual age) provided information about infant health beyond conventional clinical measures, with larger positive gaps associated with better developmental outcomes. These models offer new quantitative tools for characterizing atypical brain development in at-risk neonatal populations.

title: Aperiodic parameters of the fMRI power spectrum associate with preterm birth and neonatal age
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
institution: Developing Human Connectome Project consortium
statistical_approach: Aperiodic BOLD signal parameterization (0.01-0.15 Hz), 90 cortical/subcortical ROIs, machine learning regression, dHCP dataset
abstract: This study parameterized aperiodic components of resting-state fMRI BOLD signal power spectra from 90 cortical and subcortical regions of interest in neonates from the Developing Human Connectome Project. Pre- and postcentral gyri BOLD aperiodic exponent and offset were significantly associated with preterm birth and post-menstrual age. Machine learning analyses showed R² of 0.20-0.41 between aperiodic fMRI parameters and neonatal age, demonstrating feasibility for studying functional brain maturation via fMRI-based aperiodic activity.

title: Hierarchical maturation of structural brain connectomes from birth to childhood
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: Connectome mapping, machine learning, neurobiological annotation, network efficiency metrics
abstract: This study reveals hierarchical development of the brain's structural connectome from infancy to childhood using connectome mapping, machine learning, and neurobiological annotation. Two cardinal topological transformations occur: (1) nonlinear global increases in network efficiency and robustness; (2) regional reorganization with accelerated hub consolidation and prolonged modular reconfiguration in prefrontal and insular cortices. Early developmental trajectories of association cortices predict late childhood network architecture through local microstructural maturation of connected white matter tracts, characterized by distinct sensorimotor-association developmental trajectories.

title: Longitudinal White Matter Maturation in Preterm Infants: Functional Pathway-Specific Trajectories and Associations with Motor Outcomes
authors: Gang Yi Lee, Yong Hun Jang, Joo Young Lee, Hyuna Kim, Bong Gun Lee, Mi Jung Kim, Hyun Ju Lee
institution: Samsung Medical Center / Sungkyunkwan University, Seoul, South Korea
statistical_approach: Linear mixed models for longitudinal DTI, 26 functionally distinct white matter pathways, 174 scans (preterm n=58, full-term n=23), Bayley-III motor scores at 2 years
abstract: This longitudinal study used diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) to characterize development of 26 functionally distinct white matter pathways in preterm and full-term infants across the first two years of life (174 total scans). Significant group differences in developmental trajectories were observed in pathways associated with motor function, visual processing, and cognition. The middle cerebellar peduncle showed the strongest association with motor outcomes at 2 years, suggesting that pathway-specific white matter maturation patterns can serve as early biomarkers for neurodevelopmental outcome prediction in preterm populations.

title: Cross-paradigm fNIRS brain activity in 1-month-old infants across The Gambia and the United Kingdom
authors: Isobel Greenhalgh, Borja Blanco, Chiara Bulgarelli, Ebrima Mbye, Ebou Touray, Maria Rozhko, Laura Katus, Nathan Hayes, Samantha McCann, Sophie E. Moore, Clare E. Elwell, Anna Blasi, Sarah Lloyd-Fox
institution: University of Cambridge
statistical_approach: fNIRS channel-level analysis, cross-cultural comparison, linear mixed models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This cross-cultural study examines fNIRS-measured brain activity in 1-month-old infants across two markedly different environments — The Gambia and the United Kingdom. Using multiple paradigms (social selectivity, habituation/novelty, and functional connectivity), the authors characterize neural responses to social and non-social stimuli across these culturally and environmentally distinct populations. Findings reveal both universal patterns and cross-cultural differences in early brain function, contributing to understanding of how environment shapes early neural development.
key_figure: fNIRS activation maps across paradigms for Gambian vs. UK infant cohorts

title: Deciphering multiway multiscale brain network connectivity from birth to 6 months
authors: Qiang Li, Zening Fu, Hasse Walum, Masoud Seraji, Prerana Bajracharya, Vince D. Calhoun, Sarah Shultz, Armin Iraji
institution: Georgia State University / Emory University / TReNDS Center
statistical_approach: High-order triadic interactions, pairwise vs. beyond-pairwise connectivity, 71 infants (126 scans), functional network connectivity
abstract: This study characterized multiway multiscale brain network interactions in 71 typically developing infants during their first six months of life. High-order triadic interactions revealed significant hierarchical, multiway, multiscale brain functional network interactions beyond pairwise connectivity. These triadic interactions predominantly involve the default mode, sensorimotor, visual, limbic, language, salience, and central executive domains—aligning with the classical triple network model—suggesting the core brain network architecture is initially established during the first six months of infancy. High-order interactions provided more reliable biomarkers of developmental trajectories than pairwise measures.

title: Singing to the newborn brain uncovers early traces of specialized neural networks
authors: Marino, C., Gemignani, J., Varnet, L., and co-authors
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: fNIRS hemodynamic response analysis, within-subject contrasts (speech vs. song vs. humming), N=40 newborns
abstract: This fNIRS study with 40 newborns examined neural responses to speech, song, and humming. Stronger responses for both speech and song (vs. humming) were found in right temporoparietal regions, demonstrating early traces of specialized neural networks for communication. No significant neural differences were found between speech and song despite clear acoustic distinctions, consistent with the hypothesis that song, by regularizing speech rhythm, may support language acquisition in early development.

title: Monitoring neonatal brain function: recent advances in functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS)
authors: Fei Xu, Jie Li, Chao Zheng, Lanlan Mi
institution: Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, China
statistical_approach: Narrative review; synthesizes technical principles and clinical applications of fNIRS for neonatal brain monitoring at the bedside
abstract: This narrative review provides a comprehensive overview of recent advances in functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) for monitoring neonatal brain function. The review covers technical principles of fNIRS, applications in developmental assessment of cortical oxygenation and hemodynamics, monitoring of pathological conditions including hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy and seizures, and future translational potential in neonatal intensive care. The paper emphasizes the value of combining fNIRS with EEG, MRI, and ultrasound for integrated multimodal neonatal brain monitoring.

title: Income insufficiency impacts early brain development in infants facing increased psychosocial adversity: A network-based approach
authors: Haerin Chung, Carol L. Wilkinson, A. Liu, A. Job Said, B. Francis, G. Cañaveral, K. Conroy, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Charles A. Nelson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
statistical_approach: Partial correlation network analysis, longitudinal EEG power analysis, graph-theoretic network metrics
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study uses a network-based approach to examine how income insufficiency and psychosocial adversity affect early brain development in infants. Longitudinal EEG data from 4 to 12 months reveals that income-related adversity is associated with altered resting-state oscillatory network topology, with implications for understanding the neural mechanisms by which socioeconomic factors shape infant brain development.
key_figure: Network connectivity matrices comparing income-sufficient versus income-insufficient groups across development

title: Resting state aperiodic and periodic EEG activity in preschool-aged autistic children: differences from neurotypical peers and links to language skills
authors: Yanru Chen, Meagan Tsou, Charles A. Nelson, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Carol L. Wilkinson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
statistical_approach: Spectral parameterization (specparam/FOOOF), aperiodic exponent/offset, periodic peaks, group comparison (64 ASD, 64 neurotypical, ages 2-6), regression on language skills
abstract: This study examined resting-state aperiodic and periodic EEG features in 64 neurotypical and 64 autistic children aged 2-6 years. Aperiodic and periodic features from absolute power spectra were compared between groups and linked to concurrent language skills, with a subgroup analysis of children with language impairment. Both aperiodic and periodic EEG features showed group differences between autistic and neurotypical preschoolers, and were significantly associated with concurrent language ability. The findings suggest spectral EEG components may reflect neural mechanisms underlying language development in autism.

title: Simultaneous EEG-fMRI investigation of sound sequence processing in human neonates
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: EEG mismatch response analysis, fMRI BOLD contrasts, auditory oddball paradigm (n=25 full-term neonates, 22 analyzable)
abstract: This study acquired simultaneous EEG and fMRI data in 25 full-term sleeping neonates during an auditory oddball paradigm investigating sound sequence processing. EEG mismatch response was identified for deviant sounds but not omissions, suggesting neonates encode local but not global sequence regularity. fMRI revealed distributed cortical activity including superior and ventromedial temporal lobe (hippocampus, parahippocampus), precentral gyrus, thalamus, and parahippocampal cortex. This simultaneous multimodal approach provides comprehensive spatial and temporal insight into sensory deviance detection in the neonatal brain.

title: Language exposure predicts infants' neural processing of others' actions based on language group
authors: Yiyi Wang, Marc Colomer, Hyesung Grace Hwang, Enda Tan, Nathan A Fox
institution: University of Chicago
statistical_approach: Mu desynchronization analysis, frontal theta power, frontal alpha asymmetry, mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study investigates how early language exposure shapes infants' neural responses to others' goal-directed actions. Using EEG with 8- to 12-month-old infants from different language groups, the authors measure mu desynchronization (motor resonance), frontal theta, and frontal alpha asymmetry during action observation. Results indicate that language group moderates neural processing of actions, suggesting that early linguistic experience influences the neural systems underlying social cognition and action understanding.
key_figure: EEG mu desynchronization topographic maps by language group during action observation

title: Changes in Early Aperiodic EEG Activity Are Linked to Autism Diagnosis and Language Development in Infants With Family History of Autism
authors: Carol L. Wilkinson, Haerin Chung, Amy Dave, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Charles A. Nelson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
statistical_approach: Longitudinal EEG aperiodic change (3-12 months), regression on ASD diagnosis and 18-month language outcomes
abstract: This study examined longitudinal changes in aperiodic EEG activity from 3 to 12 months in infants with elevated familial likelihood for autism. Infants with a later autism diagnosis showed significantly increased aperiodic activity change compared to elevated-familial-likelihood infants without diagnosis. Greater increases in aperiodic offset and slope from 3-12 months were associated with worse 18-month language development, even after controlling for autism diagnosis. These results suggest dynamic changes in aperiodic EEG serve as early indicators of autism and language outcomes in high-risk infants.
